content.assessment: A transparency and evidence-infrastructure proposal is comparatively low-threat politically because it does not itself mandate closures or consolidations, potentially attracting broader coalition support (including from those opposing reorganization) while still building institutional capacity. Its central feasibility risk is not opposition but neglect: an information-only body could be politically deprioritized or bypassed by decision-makers who have historically acted on discretionary or top-down grounds rather than evidence.
opportunities: Could be integrated into an active government narrative (Magyar Falu Program) as a monitoring/accountability layer for rural development spending, gaining political sponsorship without threatening any specific village (F-live-political_feasibility-04)
strengths: Non-mandating design plausibly lowers opposition intensity relative to consolidation-oriented proposals, since no village is guaranteed closure or forced restructuring by the registry itself
Could appeal to a wide coalition, since improved transparency and cost-visibility serves the interests of both pro-consolidation reformers and pro-retention local advocates equally
threats: Long historical record of top-down political decisions overriding institutionalized processes (repeated regime-driven swings in school-network structure) suggests a purely informational registry risks being sidelined whenever political actors prefer discretionary action (F-live-political_feasibility-09)

Chronic dysfunction and deficits in the existing centralized administrative body (KLIK) raise doubts about the institutional capacity and funding priority available to sustain a rigorous, independently audited registry (F-live-political_feasibility-06)

An organized, already-mobilized education coalition (unions/parents/students) might view a registry as insufficient or as a delay tactic if not paired with substantive commitments, limiting its capacity to defuse political conflict (F-live-political_feasibility-08)
weaknesses: The proposal creates no binding decision rule, so it does not directly resolve the reversal-prone, protest-driven dynamics already observed in Hungarian closure politics (F-live-political_feasibility-07)
